questions.question: What is a bulkhead trying to control?
options: Which traffic, tenants, or features are allowed to fail together instead of sharing one blast radius.
Which code style rules apply to production services.
How often databases replicate across regions.
Whether retries should use fixed or exponential backoff.
questions.explanation: Bulkheads are about failure isolation boundaries.

questions.question: Why can one shared worker pool be risky in a mixed-priority system?
options: One noisy feature or tenant can consume capacity and drown critical work.
It guarantees lower latency than any isolated pool.
It makes observability impossible.
It prevents any use of autoscaling.
questions.explanation: Shared pools maximize efficiency until a hotspot turns them into shared failure.

questions.question: Which resource is often worth isolating first in a reliability design?
options: The execution pool or queue that carries critical traffic through overload and feature spikes.
The team calendar used for incident reviews.
The markdown formatter used by documentation builds.
The naming scheme for dashboards.
questions.explanation: Execution pools and queues directly control whether one class of work can starve another.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of dedicated capacity for critical traffic?
options: You reduce blast radius, but some capacity may sit idle in quiet periods.
You remove every need for quotas or routing policy.
You guarantee that failover never overloads remaining cells.
You make observability simpler because all metrics collapse into one pool.
questions.explanation: Isolation often costs some average efficiency in exchange for better resilience.

questions.question: Why is it dangerous if a fallback path uses the same shared dependency as the primary path?
options: The fallback may fail under the same incident, so the blast radius is not actually reduced.
The primary path will automatically recover faster.
It makes capacity borrowing unnecessary.
It guarantees eventual consistency for all data.
questions.explanation: Isolation only works when the supposedly separate path is meaningfully separate.

questions.question: Which metric best shows whether one tenant or feature is breaching its isolation boundary?
options: Per-pool utilization and reject rate diverging sharply by tenant or traffic class.
The number of interview questions in the course roadmap.
Average laptop battery health of on-call engineers.
The total count of services deployed company-wide.
questions.explanation: Per-class pool metrics reveal whether one group is dominating shared capacity.

questions.question: Why can cell-based isolation help in large fleets?
options: A failure can stay bounded to one cell or POP group instead of poisoning the whole fleet.
It removes all need for spare capacity planning.
It guarantees every request stays strongly consistent globally.
It eliminates routing complexity.
questions.explanation: Cell boundaries limit the spread of local faults, though they add placement complexity.

questions.question: What weakens a bulkhead answer most in an interview?
options: Talking about 'isolation' only abstractly without naming which resource, pool, or failure domain is actually separated.
Explaining that one huge tenant may need stronger isolation than the median tenant.
Noting that dedicated pools can lower average utilization.
Discussing revocable capacity borrowing as one compromise.
questions.explanation: Interview signal comes from concrete isolation boundaries, not only vocabulary.
